question_text: Study the diagram showing the structure of the Indian Financial Market. According to the diagram, which two main divisions does the Financial Market have?
image_alt: A tree diagram titled 'Indian Financial Market' at the top. Two main branches extend downward: Left branch — 'Money Market' (short-term, up to 1 year) with sub-branches: Treasury Bills, Commercial Paper, Call Money, Certificate of Deposit. Right branch — 'Capital Market' (long-term, more than 1 year) with sub-branches splitting into 'Primary Market (New Issues)' and 'Secondary Market (Stock Exchange)'. Each node is colour-coded with brief descriptions.
explanation: The diagram clearly shows two main divisions of the Indian Financial Market: Money Market (for short-term funds, up to 1 year) and Capital Market (for long-term funds, more than 1 year). These are the two fundamental segments through which funds flow between savers and borrowers.
subject: cuet-business-studies

elimination_hints.hint: Primary and Secondary Markets are sub-divisions of the Capital Market, not the two main divisions of the Financial Market.
Stock Exchange is part of the Secondary Market. Commodity Exchange is not shown in the NCERT classification.
Debt and Derivatives markets are not the two main divisions shown in the NCERT diagram.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using advanced classification instead of basic NCERT structure

question_text: Study the comparison table of Money Market instruments. According to the table, which instrument has the shortest maturity period?
image_alt: A comparison table titled 'Money Market Instruments' with 4 columns: Instrument, Issuer, Maturity, Minimum Amount. Rows: (1) Treasury Bills — RBI on behalf of Govt, 14/91/182/364 days, Rs 25,000. (2) Commercial Paper — Large corporates, 15 days to 1 year, Rs 5 lakh. (3) Call Money — Banks, 1 day to 15 days, No fixed minimum. (4) Certificate of Deposit — Banks/FIs, 91 days to 1 year, Rs 1 lakh. Each row has a different colour background.
explanation: According to the table, Call Money has the shortest maturity period — from just 1 day to 15 days. It is an overnight or very short-term borrowing between banks. When lent for 1 day it is 'call money' and when for more than 1 day but less than 15 days, it is 'notice money'.
subject: cuet-business-studies

elimination_hints.hint: Treasury Bills start at 14 days, but Call Money starts at just 1 day — making it shorter.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing T-Bills with the shortest instrument

elimination_hints.hint: Commercial Paper minimum maturity is 15 days — much longer than Call Money's 1 day.
Certificate of Deposit starts at 91 days — the longest minimum maturity among all listed instruments.
elimination_hints.misconception: Selecting the longest instead of shortest

question_text: Study the flowchart showing how shares are traded on the Stock Exchange. According to the diagram, what happens immediately AFTER the broker matches a buy and sell order?
image_alt: A vertical flowchart titled 'Trading Procedure on a Stock Exchange' with 6 steps: Step 1 — Investor opens Demat Account + Trading Account with a broker. Step 2 — Investor places order (buy/sell) through broker. Step 3 — Broker matches buy and sell orders on exchange. Step 4 — Trade confirmation sent to both parties. Step 5 — Settlement: Clearing house settles trades (T+2 days). Step 6 — Shares credited to buyer's Demat account, money debited. Each step has an arrow flowing to the next.
explanation: According to the flowchart, after the broker matches buy and sell orders on the exchange (Step 3), the next step is Trade Confirmation — confirmation of the trade is sent to both the buyer and seller (Step 4). Only after confirmation does settlement begin.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Shares are immediately credited to buyer's Demat account
Trade confirmation is sent to both parties
Investor opens a new Demat account
Broker places a new order on behalf of the investor

elimination_hints.hint: Shares are credited only at the final step (Step 6) after settlement — not immediately after matching.
Opening a Demat account is Step 1 — it happens before any trading, not after order matching.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the first step with a later step

elimination_hints.hint: Placing an order is Step 2. After matching (Step 3), the trade is confirmed, not re-ordered.
